title: Shoulder Dystocia
oneLiner: Obstetric emergency when the fetal shoulder is wedged behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the head – will cause fetal hypoxia and maternal trauma if not resolved rapidly.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Fetal asphyxia/hypoxic brain injury:** The umbilical cord is compressed; the clock runs from head delivery. Every minute without delivery increases risk.  
  *Key finding:* Delayed or absent breathing/tonus at birth → need immediate neonatal resuscitation team.

- **Maternal postpartum haemorrhage (PPH):** Vaginal/uterine trauma, atony after prolonged traction, or uterine rupture during manoeuvres.  
  *Key finding:* Continued heavy bleeding after delivery of the placenta, rising pulse, falling BP.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal signs:** After delivery of the fetal head:
  - Head retracts back against the perineum (“turtle sign”)
  - Restitution (rotation of head to occiput-transverse or occiput-anterior) does **not** occur
  - External rotation of head does **not** happen
  - Chin remains tight against the perineum – “face not on the floor”
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Fetal head delivered but anterior shoulder cannot be delivered with gentle downward traction
  - No crowning of shoulders
  - May see meconium (if previously clear) due to vagal stimulation
  - Maternal distress, inability to push effectively
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** True shoulder dystocia (bony impaction)  
  *Differentiator:* Turtle sign + failure of external rotation + firm resistance on gentle axial traction.
- **Less likely:** Prolonged second stage with malposition (e.g., face presentation, compound presentation)  
  *Differentiator:* Head may be in odd position but does **not** retract; traction yields progress.
- **Rare:** Fetal macrosomia with no true dystocia (resolves with positioning)  
  *Differentiator:* Gentle traction + McRoberts usually delivers.
- **Splitter:** The presence of a “turtle sign” (head retraction) is **specific** for shoulder dystocia. If absent, consider other causes of obstructed aftercoming head.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Immediately:** Call for help (midwife, senior obstetrician, anaesthetist, neonatal team)
  - **Time the event:** Document head-to-body delivery interval (using a watch)
  - **Assess neonatal tone and breathing** as soon as delivered
  - **Estimate blood loss** after delivery – visual wastage; note if >500 mL
- **Bloods (post-delivery):**
  - FBC, coagulation screen, crossmatch (if massive haemorrhage anticipated)
  - Blood gas from umbilical cord (arterial and venous) to document degree of acidaemia
- **Imaging (rarely acute):**  
  - Neonatal – X-ray clavicle/humerus if crepitus or asymmetrical moro reflex (fracture, brachial plexus injury)
  - Maternal – X-ray or CT if uterine rupture suspected (free gas, contrast extravasation)
- **Special tests:** None in the acute moment. Later – neonatal neuropraxia assessment.
- **Diagnostic criteria (clinical):**  
  The *RCOG* definition: “the need for additional obstetric manoeuvres to deliver the shoulders after the head has been delivered and gentle downward traction has failed.”
  - No scoring system used; it is a **clinical diagnosis**.
- **Never-miss test:**  
  **Document the exact head-to-body time** – this is the single most important variable for medicolegal and quality improvement. Omission is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / room):**
  - **Stop fundal pressure** – will worsen impaction (increases uterine contraction forces)
  - **Call for help** – senior obstetrician, anaesthetist, neonatal team (do not leave the room)
  - **Apply the HELPERR mnemonic stepwise:**
    - **H** — Help (call team)
    - **E** — Evaluate for episiotomy (only if needed to free hand; not routine)
    - **L** — Legs: McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflex maternal thighs onto abdomen – flattens lumbar lordosis, rotates symphysis)
    - **P** — Pressure: suprapubic pressure (not fundal) to push fetal shoulder downward and adduct
    - **E** — Enter: internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, posterior arm extraction)
    - **R** — Roll: all-fours position (Gaskin manoeuvre) if easily turned; this commonly releases posterior shoulder
  - If still unsuccessful: consider **Zavanelli manoeuvre** (replace head into uterus and proceed to caesarean) – only with senior obstetrician present
  - **Tocolysis** – terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneous (if uterine contraction is hindering manoeuvres; [check local policy] for dose) – only after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** Not applicable – patient remains in delivery suite or theatre. If delivered vaginally, **monitor for PPH:**
  - Oxytocin infusion (Syntocinon 40 IU in 500 mL NaCl 0.9% over 4 hours)
  - Watch for perineal tears, cervical lacerations → repair under regional anaesthesia
  - Check tone and lochia hourly
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Neonatal follow-up for brachial plexus palsy (physiotherapy assessment within first 2 weeks)
  - Maternal debriefing – counselling for future pregnancy (risk of recurrence ~10% if macrosomic)
- **Default Orders (if admitted to postnatal ward):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid if signs of haemorrhage; otherwise oral fluids once able
  - VTE prophylaxis: if BMI >40 or prolonged labour/VBAC, consider LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg once daily) [check local policy] after risk assessment
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if still in active haemorrhage management; otherwise free fluids then normal diet
  - Observations: maternal NEWS2 every 15 min for first hour, then hourly for 4 hours (if stable) – pay attention to HR, BP, lochia amount
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 40 IU in 500 mL NaCl 0.9% IV over 4 hours (first line for atony prevention) – **do not give IV bolus** (risk of hypotension and cardiac arrest)
- **Terbutaline:** 250 micrograms subcutaneous single dose (for uterine relaxation if McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail) [check local policy] – onset ~2–3 min, duration 15–30 min
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1 g IV over 10 min if PPH >500 mL – reduces maternal mortality (CRASH-2 trial evidence)
- **Carbetocin (long-acting oxytocin analogue):** 100 micrograms IV bolus (alternative to Syntocinon infusion) – [check local policy] for use in PPH
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
  - Terbutaline: avoid in maternal tachycardia (>120) or known cardiac disease (risk of arrhythmia)
  - Oxytocin: avoid large IV bolus in pre-existing hypotension or cardiac disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for these drugs in acute setting (short term use)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Fundal pressure** is not a drug but is the most harmful action – **do not apply**.  
  **Oxytocin IV bolus (e.g., 5–10 IU) – can cause severe hypotension and arrest.** Use infusion only for prophylaxis; bolus (if needed) should be slow and [check local policy].
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Fetal: brachial plexus injury (Erb’s palsy, Klumpke’s palsy), clavicle fracture, humeral fracture, hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy, death
  - Maternal: third- or fourth-degree perineal tear, postpartum haemorrhage, uterine rupture, symphyseal separation, anal sphincter injury
- **Chronic:**
  - Fetal: permanent brachial plexus palsy (1–2% of cases), shoulder weakness
  - Maternal: chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, faecal incontinence if sphincter damage, psychological trauma
sections.Prognosis:
- **Neonatal:** 80–90% of brachial plexus palsies resolve spontaneously within 6–12 months; persistent palsy requires surgery. Mortality <1% if delivery within 5 minutes of head delivery.  
- **Maternal:** Most recover fully, but risk of PPH is 10–15%. Uterine rupture is rare but life-threatening.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always time the head-to-body interval** – start clock as soon as head delivered; every second counts.
- **McRoberts + suprapubic pressure is the gold standard first two steps** – do not jump straight to internal manoeuvres without trying repositioning.
- **If the patient can easily turn to all-fours (Gaskin), do it** – it is often the only manoeuvre needed and improves maternal comfort.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not applied fundal pressure** – it worsens impaction and increases risk of uterine rupture and fetal fractures.
- **Do not pull on the fetal head aggressively** – axial traction only; lateral flexion will cause cervical nerve root avulsion.
- **Do not delay calling for senior help** – if initial McRoberts + pressure fail, call senior immediately; don't waste time repeating.
- **Do not forget to document every manoeuvre and their order** – medicolegal.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – r:
- **Return if:**  
  - Vaginal bleeding heavier than a heavy period or requiring pad change every hour  
  - Severe perineal pain not controlled by regular analgesia  
  - Fever, rigors, or foul-smelling lochia (endometritis)  
  - Inability to pass urine within 6 hours of delivery
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Obstetrician and midwife at 6 weeks (postnatal check)  
  - If perineal tear: perineal clinic or physiotherapy referral  
  - If neonatal brachial plexus palsy: paediatric physiotherapy within 2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior obstetrician / consultant immediately:**  
  - Failure to deliver shoulders after 2–3 manoeuvres (McRoberts + suprapubic pressure)  
  - Any suspected uterine rupture (abdominal pain, loss of station, fetal bradycardia)  
  - Maternal collapse or haemorrhage >1500 mL  
  - Need for Zavanelli manoeuvre (call consultant + anaesthetist + theatre)
- **Call neonatal team (crash call):**  
  - If head-to-body time >5 minutes (likely cord pH <7.0)  
  - Low APGAR (<7 at 5 minutes) or absent respiratory effort  
  - Meconium-stained liquor with depressed neonate
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (maternal) – escalate to registrar  
  - Lactate from cord gas >8 mmol/L or base deficit >12 mmol/L – refer to neonatology  
  - Fetal pulse oximetry (if available) <30% sustained – immediate action

rawMarkdown:
## Shoulder Dystocia

**One-liner:** Obstetric emergency when the fetal shoulder is wedged behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the head – will cause fetal hypoxia and maternal trauma if not resolved rapidly.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Fetal asphyxia/hypoxic brain injury:** The umbilical cord is compressed; the clock runs from head delivery. Every minute without delivery increases risk.  
  *Key finding:* Delayed or absent breathing/tonus at birth → need immediate neonatal resuscitation team.

- **Maternal postpartum haemorrhage (PPH):** Vaginal/uterine trauma, atony after prolonged traction, or uterine rupture during manoeuvres.  
  *Key finding:* Continued heavy bleeding after delivery of the placenta, rising pulse, falling BP.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal signs:** After delivery of the fetal head:
  - Head retracts back against the perineum (“turtle sign”)
  - Restitution (rotation of head to occiput-transverse or occiput-anterior) does **not** occur
  - External rotation of head does **not** happen
  - Chin remains tight against the perineum – “face not on the floor”
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Fetal head delivered but anterior shoulder cannot be delivered with gentle downward traction
  - No crowning of shoulders
  - May see meconium (if previously clear) due to vagal stimulation
  - Maternal distress, inability to push effectively

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely:** True shoulder dystocia (bony impaction)  
  *Differentiator:* Turtle sign + failure of external rotation + firm resistance on gentle axial traction.
- **Less likely:** Prolonged second stage with malposition (e.g., face presentation, compound presentation)  
  *Differentiator:* Head may be in odd position but does **not** retract; traction yields progress.
- **Rare:** Fetal macrosomia with no true dystocia (resolves with positioning)  
  *Differentiator:* Gentle traction + McRoberts usually delivers.
- **Splitter:** The presence of a “turtle sign” (head retraction) is **specific** for shoulder dystocia. If absent, consider other causes of obstructed aftercoming head.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - **Immediately:** Call for help (midwife, senior obstetrician, anaesthetist, neonatal team)
  - **Time the event:** Document head-to-body delivery interval (using a watch)
  - **Assess neonatal tone and breathing** as soon as delivered
  - **Estimate blood loss** after delivery – visual wastage; note if >500 mL
- **Bloods (post-delivery):**
  - FBC, coagulation screen, crossmatch (if massive haemorrhage anticipated)
  - Blood gas from umbilical cord (arterial and venous) to document degree of acidaemia
- **Imaging (rarely acute):**  
  - Neonatal – X-ray clavicle/humerus if crepitus or asymmetrical moro reflex (fracture, brachial plexus injury)
  - Maternal – X-ray or CT if uterine rupture suspected (free gas, contrast extravasation)
- **Special tests:** None in the acute moment. Later – neonatal neuropraxia assessment.
- **Diagnostic criteria (clinical):**  
  The *RCOG* definition: “the need for additional obstetric manoeuvres to deliver the shoulders after the head has been delivered and gentle downward traction has failed.”
  - No scoring system used; it is a **clinical diagnosis**.
- **Never-miss test:**  
  **Document the exact head-to-body time** – this is the single most important variable for medicolegal and quality improvement. Omission is a serious error.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / room):**
  - **Stop fundal pressure** – will worsen impaction (increases uterine contraction forces)
  - **Call for help** – senior obstetrician, anaesthetist, neonatal team (do not leave the room)
  - **Apply the HELPERR mnemonic stepwise:**
    - **H** — Help (call team)
    - **E** — Evaluate for episiotomy (only if needed to free hand; not routine)
    - **L** — Legs: McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflex maternal thighs onto abdomen – flattens lumbar lordosis, rotates symphysis)
    - **P** — Pressure: suprapubic pressure (not fundal) to push fetal shoulder downward and adduct
    - **E** — Enter: internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, posterior arm extraction)
    - **R** — Roll: all-fours position (Gaskin manoeuvre) if easily turned; this commonly releases posterior shoulder
  - If still unsuccessful: consider **Zavanelli manoeuvre** (replace head into uterus and proceed to caesarean) – only with senior obstetrician present
  - **Tocolysis** – terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneous (if uterine contraction is hindering manoeuvres; [check local policy] for dose) – only after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** Not applicable – patient remains in delivery suite or theatre. If delivered vaginally, **monitor for PPH:**
  - Oxytocin infusion (Syntocinon 40 IU in 500 mL NaCl 0.9% over 4 hours)
  - Watch for perineal tears, cervical lacerations → repair under regional anaesthesia
  - Check tone and lochia hourly
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Neonatal follow-up for brachial plexus palsy (physiotherapy assessment within first 2 weeks)
  - Maternal debriefing – counselling for future pregnancy (risk of recurrence ~10% if macrosomic)
- **Default Orders (if admitted to postnatal ward):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid if signs of haemorrhage; otherwise oral fluids once able
  - VTE prophylaxis: if BMI >40 or prolonged labour/VBAC, consider LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg once daily) [check local policy] after risk assessment
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if still in active haemorrhage management; otherwise free fluids then normal diet
  - Observations: maternal NEWS2 every 15 min for first hour, then hourly for 4 hours (if stable) – pay attention to HR, BP, lochia amount

### Drugs & Doses

- **Oxytocin (Syntocinon):** 40 IU in 500 mL NaCl 0.9% IV over 4 hours (first line for atony prevention) – **do not give IV bolus** (risk of hypotension and cardiac arrest)
- **Terbutaline:** 250 micrograms subcutaneous single dose (for uterine relaxation if McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail) [check local policy] – onset ~2–3 min, duration 15–30 min
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1 g IV over 10 min if PPH >500 mL – reduces maternal mortality (CRASH-2 trial evidence)
- **Carbetocin (long-acting oxytocin analogue):** 100 micrograms IV bolus (alternative to Syntocinon infusion) – [check local policy] for use in PPH
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
  - Terbutaline: avoid in maternal tachycardia (>120) or known cardiac disease (risk of arrhythmia)
  - Oxytocin: avoid large IV bolus in pre-existing hypotension or cardiac disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for these drugs in acute setting (short term use)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Fundal pressure** is not a drug but is the most harmful action – **do not apply**.  
  **Oxytocin IV bolus (e.g., 5–10 IU) – can cause severe hypotension and arrest.** Use infusion only for prophylaxis; bolus (if needed) should be slow and [check local policy].

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Fetal: brachial plexus injury (Erb’s palsy, Klumpke’s palsy), clavicle fracture, humeral fracture, hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy, death
  - Maternal: third- or fourth-degree perineal tear, postpartum haemorrhage, uterine rupture, symphyseal separation, anal sphincter injury
- **Chronic:**
  - Fetal: permanent brachial plexus palsy (1–2% of cases), shoulder weakness
  - Maternal: chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, faecal incontinence if sphincter damage, psychological trauma

### Prognosis

- **Neonatal:** 80–90% of brachial plexus palsies resolve spontaneously within 6–12 months; persistent palsy requires surgery. Mortality <1% if delivery within 5 minutes of head delivery.  
- **Maternal:** Most recover fully, but risk of PPH is 10–15%. Uterine rupture is rare but life-threatening.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always time the head-to-body interval** – start clock as soon as head delivered; every second counts.
- **McRoberts + suprapubic pressure is the gold standard first two steps** – do not jump straight to internal manoeuvres without trying repositioning.
- **If the patient can easily turn to all-fours (Gaskin), do it** – it is often the only manoeuvre needed and improves maternal comfort.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not applied fundal pressure** – it worsens impaction and increases risk of uterine rupture and fetal fractures.
- **Do not pull on the fetal head aggressively** – axial traction only; lateral flexion will cause cervical nerve root avulsion.
- **Do not delay calling for senior help** – if initial McRoberts + pressure fail, call senior immediately; don't waste time repeating.
- **Do not forget to document every manoeuvre and their order** – medicolegal.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – rare, but after PPH recovery)

- **Return if:**  
  - Vaginal bleeding heavier than a heavy period or requiring pad change every hour  
  - Severe perineal pain not controlled by regular analgesia  
  - Fever, rigors, or foul-smelling lochia (endometritis)  
  - Inability to pass urine within 6 hours of delivery
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Obstetrician and midwife at 6 weeks (postnatal check)  
  - If perineal tear: perineal clinic or physiotherapy referral  
  - If neonatal brachial plexus palsy: paediatric physiotherapy within 2 weeks

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior obstetrician / consultant immediately:**  
  - Failure to deliver shoulders after 2–3 manoeuvres (McRoberts + suprapubic pressure)  
  - Any suspected uterine rupture (abdominal pain, loss of station, fetal bradycardia)  
  - Maternal collapse or haemorrhage >1500 mL  
  - Need for Zavanelli manoeuvre (call consultant + anaesthetist + theatre)
- **Call neonatal team (crash call):**  
  - If head-to-body time >5 minutes (likely cord pH <7.0)  
  - Low APGAR (<7 at 5 minutes) or absent respiratory effort  
  - Meconium-stained liquor with depressed neonate
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (maternal) – escalate to registrar  
  - Lactate from cord gas >8 mmol/L or base deficit >12 mmol/L – refer to neonatology  
  - Fetal pulse oximetry (if available) <30% sustained – immediate action
